replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On 1), a couple of suggestions:

1a) I have always felt a distinct difference between imagining "making" a sound and "hearing" one. Making a sound in my imagination feels like the same thing as my inner monologue, and is much easier than imagining hearing something. To work on "hearing", I used to imagine a small bit of music that I knew well enough to vividly imagine hearing. Picking something instrumental and complex helped eliminate mentally "humming" it or "singing along". I found classical useful for this, especially full symphonic pieces - rather unoriginally, the fourth movement of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony (the Ode to Joy) has always been one of my favorites.

1b) To develop the faculty of paying attention to hearing specifically, I used to purposely immerse myself in a complex soundscape, and then try to isolate and progressively add together separate pieces of that sound. One example: if listening to a piece of music, I would pay attention to the percussion, and then to the bass strings, and then to the tenor strings, and then vocals, and so forth as they got added into the piece. Second example: if in the shower, I might try to focus first on the sound of the water hitting my head/body, and then next hitting the tub, and then on the water going down the drain, and so on. The goal was to try to maintain the distinction between each "separate" sound, while progressively getting a more complete aural "picture". Eventually, I found this helped with 1a).

I haven't intentionally pursued either in some years, but I hope this helps and let me know if you have any questions.

Thanks,
Jeff

comments.title: Tree "Creed" and Cantref Correspondences in Dolmen Arch
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone's December is off to a good start and that your plans are near full-waxing along with the moon.

To Share: I've been working on this for a while now, and I'm not quite sure I've got it yet, but in the interest of pushing myself, I'm sharing here now that I've got a version that might be done. I'm working on a "Heathen Rosary", and the approach I'm taking is to come up with an equivalent piece for each piece of the traditional Catholic Rosary. In the stead of the Apostle's Creed, I wanted a statement not of belief, but of intention and practice, to better match how I see polytheistic religion. The form is "Anglo-Saxon Long Lines", the words all grew out of Old English, and much of the imagery (including the central metaphor) grew out of Anglo-Saxon-flavored Heathenry, but I think the poem itself might work for a broad range of polytheists. At any rate, I hope it at least serves as inspiration to some, and I'd love to hear any feedback folks have, as I'm still new to this whole poetry business:


Hallowward we have | our hearts foreturned
to give worship | to wealsome Gods,
watering anew | that nut life-bearing.

Bright with holiness | our hearts become,
as troth we give | to trustworthy Gods
in fair trade upon | tree life-binding.

Shining happily | our hearts should be,
for steering will be given | by steadfast Gods
to bless us with | blossoms life-healing.

comments.title: Agnihotra ritual
comments.content:
Good morning JMG and everybody else! I hope y‘all had a wonderful weekend and a pleasant start into the week. A special thanks to you, JMG, for hosting us and dealing with all the questions! :-)

Somebody I happen to know recently mentioned an „Agnihotra ritual“ and offered to explain/show it. This was in particular about the benefits of the ritual and/or of the ashes to the fertility and resilience of the land.

Two questions about that:

1. Does this ritual have any kind of spiritual and/or occult implications? Could there be any unwanted side effects or long-term effects from such a ritual?

In particular, if somebody was following any of the occult/magic paths you are teaching, could there be any cross-effects?

I‘m a bit hesitant to perform a ritual if I don’t really know what I‘m doing there, let alone what subtle effects it might have.

2. After the recent Open Post discussion about the Temple Secret, I found it quite curious that the Agnihotra ritual is being marketed as good for the fertility of the land, the resilience of plants, reduction of pests, etc (and of course, according to some of its fans, everything else under the planet one would wish for - one of these days, I‘ll find a „spiritual technique“ which will also magically clean my bathroom! ;-) ).

Seriously, though, the Agnihotra ritual seems to combine pretty much the same elements as the temple technology, but in a slightly different way:

Geometry (in this case, pyramids and maybe circles/spheres)
Chanting (mantras)
Food offerings (rice, ghee)
Incense (not quite, but burning ghee and cow manure might have a similar effect)
Human participation through ritual

The only thing which is missing is the location - instead there is timing.

Could the Agnihotra ritual make use of the same „technique“ that the temple technology has been using? If so, comparison might give some more insights into how the technique works, and what is important to it.

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Agnihotra ritual
replies.content:
1) Yes, emphatically. The Agnihotra is a sacred ritual, reenacting the original sacrifice made by Prajapati, the first being, and invokes the fire god Agni and the sun god Surya. It isn't something ever to do without a vivid awareness of its spiritual dimension. If you're considering doing it, I'd encourage you to consult a qualified Hindu pandit to get some sense of what kind of commitment you're making and how best to handle it.

2) That's certainly a point worth following up on. Burnt offerings were standard practice in many branches of the temple tradition, of course, and it's worth following up in traditional Hindu literature about the Agnihotra.

replies.title: Re: Agnihotra ritual
replies.content:
Re 1) Thanks! I suspected as much but wanted to check back with more experienced people - instead of just not participating in a demo ritual (which could potentially have been useful) based on a mere assumption. But in this case, I'll happily skip it.

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Agnihotra ritual
replies.content:
I've had some training in fire ritual from Vajrayana Buddhist lineages, some of which still preserve fire rituals from old Indian Tantric sources. An out-of-context ritual is like an out-of-context power tool - you can get creative and do things with it, but it's best to understand what that ritual tool was exquisitely refined to do, based on thousands of years of experience within a problem-solving context.
We should also keep in mind that for many of us 'modern' people, the categories of 'sacrifice' and 'worship' are based, consciously or not, on the peculiar notions of orthodox or mainstream Christianity, not generally applicable to reality outside of their particular millenarian view of things.
The Vedic understanding of all fire rituals is strictly *do ut des.* The operator or devotee gives in order to get. Vedic fire rituals were never done simply as oblations or as general acts of adoration, they always ended with specific requests or petitions for material or spiritual effects. It seems that ancient people focused on practical or material concerns like children, crop fertility, wealth, health, or power over enemies.
The fire is the medium or doorway of an exchange - in Christian terms, it's not so much the sacrifice or Eucharist itself, it's the *priest.* That's why things can go into it. The fire can also be likened to a gateway, or a radio set. What fire re-enacts in a microcosm is the creation, sustenance, and destruction of the entire cosmos - you as the master of fire are Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva.
In thermodynamic terms, a fire is a pool of intensified entropy - you are rapidly increasing the appearance of randomness or disorder in a particular location - compare your ritual action to the entropy differentials of stars, which are our primal agnihotras.
In Tantric Buddhist versions of this ritual, we always specify mantras as well, as sonic embodiments of deities.

Regards,
Josh Rout

Or wealth? Wouldn't "more entropy" in a literal sense also mean more weeds, pests etc? Somehow I can't quite see the connection right now.

replies.title: Re: Agnihotra ritual
replies.content: Letting cabbages grow in a bed of clover reduces pests finding and eating the cabbages. Looks untidy to most eyes but also adds nitrogen to the soil. Letting  a few ‘pet weeds’ remain in each row of a field allows the vigorous weed roots to bring up moisture and minerals from a deeper layer of soil than the food crops typically reach. Looks lazy but has a purpose. Allowing shallow-rooted broadleaf weeds to carpet the rows between stalks of corn retains moisture in the soil and keeps the soil temps cooler in midday, preventing scorching. Packing productive crops into a tight space cheek by jowl, to use an animal metaphor, crowds out weed and allows companion plants to assist each other’s growth. Looks unruly, has a purpose.

The animals are born here, range the property, and are given a dignified death with a sharp knife. My hands help the lambs in their first hours, guide them to their pastures, and end their lives. My skills and behaviors continue to improve, and it is not meant to be an unemotional activity, but I can feel that the spiritual aspect of my activity is insufficient. My current thoughts are to more explicitly dedicate prayers for the animals, make offerings to the gods, and sanctify the slaughtering space. It is against perceived norms, which is why I mention it here, that I have found excellent luck with sky burial of the carcasses. The smell is non-existent compared with ground burial, and the etherics are much clearer. Anyway, any thoughts and questions are welcome. Thank You.

Decrying animal sacrifice and 'idols' is very Christian - but remember, Muslims practice the qurban sacrifice every year, Jewish Orthodox liturgy contains prayers for the restoration of sacrifices, and Samaritans do Passover sacrifices every year on Mt Gerizim; I would also mention the beautiful tradition of kourbania, Christian animal sacrifice https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kourbania
African traditional religions are spreading in the United States; some of us are priests in religions that regularly do animal sacrifices of fowl and livestock to the gods, and we are specifically trained in both the ritual act and in the physical aspects of butchering. replies.title: Re: Rosicrucian influence on your teaching
replies.content:
The material I learned from John was almost entirely a matter of practices, and the teachings included with it were all focused on guiding the practice. There wasn't a lot of philosophy.

That's the thing I've gotten from the Rosicrucian tradition. The particular school I work with was founded by George Winslow Plummer, who received a Rosicrucian initiation and a set of practices as a relatively young man and spent most of his life working out the philosophical and theological implications. replies.title: Re: Picatrix, astrological magic, and the Shem Ha Mephorash
replies.content: No, Solomonic magic hasn't been influenced by Picatrix, or vice versa. Both of them are survivals from the magic of the classical world, and so share certain features in common. They're different survivals, however, and the role of astrology in the two systems is emphatically not the same. They should not be mashed together all anyhow! If you want to work with both, choose one and master it -- as in, work through every detail of the system until you get good results reliably -- and then do the same with the other; by the time you're finished, you'll have the practical experience to be able to figure out how to find bridges between them.

comments.title: CosDoc
comments.content:
I continue to slog through the CosDoc and would like to share some of my thoughts and questions that arose. I'd be happy if you'd feel inclined to comment...

While meditating on the last parts of chapter 5 (The Genesis of a Solar System) and your comments on the chapter I had some "revelation". Fortune writes that most fail to understand the meaning of "the absolute". And then there is the description of the fractal nature of the cosmos (or creation as a whole). What is the absolute of a fractal? It is it's building rule (though one could argue that there has to be something even more fundamental). If you take the Mandelbrot set as an example, you have a function (a lens, if you will) that you repeatedly apply to the complex plane and by this process you "sculpt" the Mandelbrot set. One could say the complex plane is the unmanifest, the Mandelbrot set is manifestation and the function that creates it is the absolute, the fundamental rule whose working can be seen in every tiny part of manifestation yet can never be observed directly. In that view, the absolute can be found by observing manifestation, but never in manifestation?

Is this what defines the act of praying to an idol - to confuse the absolute with one of it's manfestations and pray to that instead? There are many historical and mythical examples, of course, but why not take one that is more recent: There is science, the method, which we could call "the absolute". Then there is Science, the white coats, the hierarchy, the founding agencies, and so on. It is one manifestation that came into being as a byproduct of the repeated application of science. Obviously, many people have decided to worship the manifestation instead of the principle.

Does that also mean that there is duality between manifestation and absolute in the divine? On the one hand, we speak of the divine very much in the sense of the absolute (or something closer to the absolute than we are), on the other hand we turn to the manifestation of certain gods to which we pray as a manifest being, much more wise and powerful than we are. Would it be correct to say that we can see gods as a part of manifestation that displays the working of the absolute in "high fidelity" and a certain way and that we can turn to a certain god as a being to be shown or to understand or to attune with the working of the absolute within him?

The CosDoc describes all of this as being more or less the first "iteration" of the absolute (principle). But at the same time it provides the context for an infinite recursion. I guess we can't say of know much about that?

And some last remark - after your last comments I finally managed to stop trying to make sense of the CosDoc in any physical way - I have to say it does make much more sense now. If I understand what you write about the matter correctly, Dion Fortune (or whoever created that text) chose physical analogies that pretend to make physical sense but ultimately don't not without purpose!?

Phew, I hope that wasn't too much...

Greetings & many thanks,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The method I learned from John Gilbert is very straightforward, and suitable for apprentice Druids of your daughter's age. First, wash the stone thoroughly in cold water and then dry it with a clean cloth or paper towel, and set it in sunlight for at least a few hours -- that will clear away any unwanted energies from the stone. 1) A couple of months ago I began a Venus working where I lit a green candle and recited the Orphic Hymn to Venus during the appropriate planetary hour and day. However, about a month ago my work schedule has changed which prevented me from doing this working at the ideal time each week. Sanctuary of the Rose and Chalice

replies.title: Re: Venus Working
replies.content:
1) There are four planetary hours for each planet during that planet's day. You ought to be able to do at least one of those. Don't abandon the working halfway through -- that's a very bad habit to get into.

2) You'll have to talk to a Wiccan about that. Wicca isn't a tradition I've ever felt drawn to study, and I don't know a great deal about it.

comments.title: Happy Panda's Russo-Ukraine War spread for 2022-2023

I'm submitting a mundane tarot spread for the proxy War of the NATO West vs. Russia in Ukraine.

I am taking JMG's advice to hold myself to account by publicly submitting my reading.


In short, I am now suspicious that my earlier 12 month U.S. spread for the final 2 months of 2022 to all of 2023 was indicating both financial and geopolitical events.


After a long Y/N session of turning over the cards to various questions I finally honed it down to a few surprising ones. The questions didn't even hit me until about 30 minutes later after reading my other spreads and trying to think hard about what they were saying.

I first did a simple 3 card spread:


Russia in December 2022 of Ukraine War Efforts


Russia Now: 9 of Wands R - the card Sri Arya says is indicative of 'bone-weary exhaustion though not to the point of paralysis'.


Where Russia is to Go: King of Pentacles - premier card of good fortune in the material plane. Whatever it is that's going to happen in December will go well for them. Shockingly well to NATO pundits and the chattering classes.


The Means: The Devil R - Sri Arya says this card R is the card of striking out hard pre-emptively due to fear. I really do feel Russia is feeling existentially threatened - to a degree Western elites don't realize and thus are willing to take measures that ratchet up the threat of NATO vs. Russia hot war directly. The cards seem to say they really do feel their very existence is threatened - better to go out standing tall than become slaves to the West on their knees. Or so it seems to me is their thinking. My guess is they have intel that NATO is planning on parking nuclear weapons in countries close to Russia's borders. Which I suppose should also worry China as I think those same weapons might be able to reach Beijing too though I didn't do any spreads on China over this NATO proxy war.



For the next round I used the following simple divination technique - Upright (U) = yes. Reverse (R) = no.


So I asked the following questions.


1. Will Russia attack Kiev?


4 of Cups - yes. Sri Arya says this card is one of tapping into inner resources for inner wisdom and for overcoming external forces. It's a card indicative of high spiritual growth as all material options get discarded. It's also, interestingly, a card warning of later complete breakdown. Whether that breakdown is indicated for Russian forces or for NATO forces I don't yet know.


2. Will Russia attack any cities other than Kiev?


The Emperor - Yes, decisively so and display to the world that it's still a military force to be reckoned with.


3. Will Russia attack Latvia, Lithuania or Estonia?


The World (B) - Nataraja - one of Sri Arya's 2 unique World arcana cards. Nataraja indicates accelerated karmic culmination. Described as the highest energy card of the deck. Turned upright it indicates things going very well. So I read this as Russia's December military moves going surprisingly well.


If Russia deliberately attacks a NATO country (as this spread indicates them doing) that means they've decided its worth the risk. Possibly because they either have intel to think they won't be decimated by a massive NATO counter-strike or alternately because they think permitting this war to limp along will only allow NATO time to strengthen their response to Russia's military adequately - especially by parking devastating weapons uncomfortably close to Russia's borders. Russia knows U.S. and EU elites are malicious and duplicitous enough to use them (see Iraq war for history's latest refresher) despite assurances they would only be used as a last resort. Scratch that...the U.S. recently reserved the right to use nukes in a first-strike. I'd get PTSD over that too if Russia or China were to credibly announce the same at the U.S. Especially after consistently seeing how unhinged mentally and emotionally the elites still in charge are against their own citizens.


I think Russia is growing weary and reaching a stage of 'do or die'. So they've decided to go all in and up the ante. It will be clear that they're calling out NATO specifically by attacking a NATO member per NATO's treaties. Maybe they're wanting NATO to look bad to non-NATO countries. My later divinations say they will succeed at showing the world NATO is either unwilling or unable (likely both) to honor the terms of the treaty to member countries other than with tiny token efforts. Yes, the cards did say NATO will respond but only with token efforts of the kind they're already doing. Not the kind of treaty-promised mass-mobilizations in NATO countries the U.S. did even as recently under Bush for the invasion of Iraq.


I reshuffled the deck several times, split them into 3 decks and regrouped them again several times, re-shuffled again, re-grouped several times again then proceeded.



I did a further spread to see if the U.S. would begin mobilizing the U.S. for wartime similar to the way it did for Iraq.


Result: 8 of cups R. No, it won't. Further all 8s are end-of-karma cards. It seems this might inspire segments of Congress to begin dialing back on supporting Ukraine with endless weapons transfers and defense contracts with no conditions attached.



Will non-NATO countries see the U.S. militarily weakening due to this Russian attack on NATO forces?


3 of cups - yes, and there will be celebrations worldwide as a result to boot.


Will de-dollarization by non-NATO countries pick up speed after this event?


The World (B) Nataraja - yes. Very strong yes since Sri Arya says it's the highest energy card of the deck. This may be part of why I think my earlier financial interpretation for the U.S. 2023 may still hold up. Sept 2023 I turned over 10 of Swords for the U.S. - the rock bottom card of the entire deck for the country. If it's 'game on' for other countries to begin shedding dollars then I can see why the economic outlook of 2023 was mostly negative for the U.S. overall.


I also did extra spreads that indicate the ordinary citizenry of the EU will be surprised and scared at Russia's military responses. Their elites will be concerned but the cards indicate they won't be convinced Russia is in as unhappy a mental state as the cards say it really is. So expect European elites, especially UK elites, to continue to bluster and threaten and put European citizen lives at stake from their own bubble havens. Poland in particular will have PTSD over it according to my spreads. I guess Germany and France should get ready for waves of Poles emigrating to their countries as the spreads I do for Poland and its geopolitical issues keeps turning over the same card indicative of waves of citizenry deciding to pack up and flee.


They may not be wrong in that assessment either judging from some other cards I've turned over. Especially if NATO decides Poland would make the next great staging ground for their proxy war against Russia. My spread on that says Polish politicians will be enticed with all kinds of goodies for the country if they agree to host more NATO troops, weapons, arms, nukes, etc. A decision will have to be made but the cards don't say which way Polish politicians will ultimately lean.


FYI: my spread readings for UK elites shows them to be particularly nasty-minded, unhinged and holding hard grudges against Russia - far, far beyond what even anti-Russian U.S. elites hold against it. I get the sense from the cards there are plenty of U.S. elites (admittedly not all) who would be willing to work something out with Russia - some sort of compromise.


UK elites though...hard NO. No compromise allowed. UK seems to be driving a lot of the non-negotiability stance of NATO with Russia. Something very weird and dark vis-a-vis Russia drives UK elites. Not sure why. I have a suspicion it may be past life karmic in nature. The pigeons are coming home to roost. UK elite's cards keep turning over quite dark. They burn white-hot to make Russia pay. Basically the cards say they're 'overwhelmingly driven' (Sri Arya's words) by the desire for vengeance. Worryingly to the point of being unable to see things pragmatically or practically when it comes to war.



Also, Armstrong's Socrates program has been indicating 2023 as a year of 'war-time economic scarcities' sweeping the global economy. If Russia is going to deliberately try to fracture NATO (which I think the cards are indicating they'll be shockingly successful at) then there's another reason the U.S. (and probably other NATO countries too) will hit rock bottom later next year.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi John,

Apologies for the long message, I would like your advice on the following please. I will be making my last trip to my family home this week. It is a very old house in rural Ireland. It has been in our family for at least 4 generations, but probably many more. None of my family has lived there for the last 15 years and I have not set foot across its threshold in all that time. We are selling it before nature fully takes it back.

I do not have any great affection for the house itself, both of my parents died when I was quiet young and I have often been dogged by the feeling that it has been a source of ill-luck for my family. I have a distinct memory of my mother, ashen with fear, claiming that she could see a banshee in the garden one night at dusk. I know that a man fell to his death from the gable end of the house many decades ago. When I was 18, I remember been gripped by a nasty bout of sleep paralysis. This one was different; in that I could sense something malevolent standing behind the half-open door of my room. This stretched on for a while until I was eventually fished out of the murk by my brother. He had woken to me to say that a classmate of mine had died during the early morning. I only slept in that house one more night and I have never gotten sleep paralysis since. I think each of my siblings have similar stories, but I have never found the right time or way to ask them.

These stories, and the sale of the family home, had not been of great concern to me and I was minded to leave it pass out of our hands without any great thought. I am a quietly content man now and I have a family of my own and so my past has receded to the corners of my mind. However, I recently had a conversation with my sister that stirred up old feelings and a new curiosity.

This sister of mine is not prone to any class of drama or exaggeration, she is sober and sensible, but she also has a preternatural instinct for events and people, and she has used this to become a very successful businessperson. She probably thinks of me as a similar type of sensible character, which would explain her surprise to find that I am learning to read tarot cards. A surprise that caused her to recount the following experience she had at that house.

What she told me she kept to herself for the best part of 30 year. She told me a man she had never met before called to our house one summers evening. This was strange in itself as our village very rarely received unknown visitors, it is off the beaten trail and modern life has a way of flowing around and not through it. My sister opened the door, and there was a small, grey-haired, out-off place character. He asked if the bar could be opened and to get my father. My sister did this, and my father, who was no stranger in dealing with odd characters (a 1980's Irish publican had the nerve of a lion-tamer) -and who would never reasonably turn down a paying customer- came to the door. He was deeply troubled by this man and sent him away. My sister was instantly gripped by the premonition that our father would die and spent an inconsolable night at a friend's house. That night, he died suddenly of a heart attack at 54 years of age.

I had been turning this story over in my mind for the last few days and then another sister came to visit me. She brought with her a set of keys to the family house fastened onto an orange tab. Now, orange is colour that I am powerfully drawn to. I can't help but feel that this was a prompt of some kind.

My question is- should I just blunder into that house as I am now, unprepared or unattuned to what might strike me? Can you offer some advice to me? I get the sense that this is a challenge and an opportunity to lay some demons (figuratively, most definitely not literally) to rest.

The older I get the more I feel that in life a challenge ducked is a challenge forestalled. I would rather make peace with the past now that have it pop up when I'm busy with something else. I would normally keep such things to myself, but then your recent post stating that 'A door will open' has stuck in my mind, and since your writing has turned me onto tarot cards and my learning tarot cards caused my sister to tell me the story that started this curiosity, it feels necessary that I should ask you about this John.

Any advice, no matter how incidental would be greatly appreciated John.
Yours sincerely,
Dermotok

The house may be trying to claim one more victim before your family gets away.

My grandfather, even though I never met him cast a long shadow, married 3 times buried 2 wives, 14 children most of whom he tried to ram into one religious order or another and many shy sons who never really spoke about him.

Dermotok

comments.title: Knife on Bus
comments.content:
I had a weird thing happen to me. In one sense nothing happened - I wasn't injured in the slightest. As I was quickly sitting down onto a bus seat I 'saw' a seatbelt, thinking, "Oh. When did they start putting seatbelts on buses?" I sat down on top of the 'seatbelt' and realized (1) none of the other seats had belts, and (2) the object felt rather strange and I pulled it out from under me and saw that it was no seatbelt but a knife, an open pocket knife. Somewhat alarmed, I quickly covered yet carefully it up with my coat. The really weird part is that the 'vibe' of the bus changed. It suddenly seemed as if ever other person on the bus was a gang-banger, looking at me - in all rather creepy and sinister. I managed to eventually wrap the knife in an old newspaper and give it to the driver. At which point I felt quite relieved, and the fellow passengers were mainly tired working class folks, a few tourists, etc. In other words, all was 'normal' again.

So, my question: Can knives carry some sort of charge, especially when used as weapons? (Of course the knife on the bus could have been a utility knife and the strange 'vibe' I felt simply a product of an overactive imagination...)

replies.title: Re: Knife on Bus
replies.content: Yes. Metal is good at holding energies, and iron or steel naturally attracts the energies of Mars -- violent and dangerous unless very carefully handled.
